title: Reply to the Comment by Anderson and Bakker on "Faulty Assumptions: Groundwater Modeling Through Anisotropic Fault Zones" by Lin and Lin (2025)

research.question: Is the transient zero-thickness interface for an anisotropic fault a defensible first-order representation, and over what fault-width, time, and anisotropy regimes does it approximate a finite-width fault zone?
research.system: A pumped aquifer-fault-aquifer system in which a finite-width anisotropic fault is reduced to an imperfect zero-thickness interface carrying drawdown and normal-flux jumps.
research.inputData: Analytical drawdown fields for fault-width ratios 0.1, 1, and 10, anisotropy ratios 0.1, 1, and 10, dimensionless times 10, 10^3, and 10^6, plus late-time fields at dimensionless time 10^12.
research.method: Re-derive the interface from a first-order thin-layer expansion, show how the cross-fault gradient is retained through a cross-sectional average, and benchmark the interface solution against a transient full two-dimensional three-zone reference.
research.benchmark: The full two-dimensional transient aquifer-fault-aquifer solution, with the Anderson (2006) steady-state solution included in late-time comparisons.
research.uncertainty: Approximation error is expressed as RMSE and as kernel-density distributions of absolute relative drawdown error, with 5% and 10% engineering tolerances.
research.conclusion: The interface and Anderson formulations are first-order thin-layer approximations; the transient interface generally stays within 5-10% for faults no wider than the well-to-fault distance, while thick faults and very early strongly anisotropic transients can require an explicit two-dimensional model.

narrative.throughline: Collapse a finite anisotropic fault into an interface, then make the discarded thickness visible as a measured error rather than an unexamined assumption.
narrative.audienceStartingPoint: The audience can read pumping-induced drawdown contours and understands that a narrow fault can redirect groundwater flow.
narrative.stakes: Using an interface outside its thin-layer regime can bias near-fault drawdown, whereas always resolving the fault as a two-dimensional subdomain adds unnecessary complexity.
narrative.resolution: The derivation identifies the approximation order and the benchmark maps the practical boundary between interface and explicit-fault models.

background.premise: The comment raises three issues concerning the scope, steady-state equivalence, and unquantified transient error of the anisotropic-fault interface.
background.visibleEvidence: Three callouts attach to the same fault cross-section: wording scope, conductive-fault equivalence, and approximation error.
background.whyNow: These are the three questions the reply must resolve before its derivation or benchmark has meaning.
background.boundary: The critique is restricted to transient anisotropic fault scenarios, not all earlier interface formulas.
background.sourceExcerpt: The comment raises three issues: first, our use of the phrase
background.visualObject: A pumped aquifer split by a finite fault, with three question markers anchored to the interface.
background.motionPurpose: Reveal the three challenged claims one at a time on a single scientific object.
background.stepDetail: Show the well and fault; expose the scope question; add the steady conductive limit; finish with an empty error gauge.
background.transitionBridge: The empty error gauge becomes the quantitative target of the later benchmark.

background.premise: A thin layer can be represented by a zero-thickness fault interface that retains leading-order fault-width effects and omits higher-order contributions.
background.visibleEvidence: A finite fault strip narrows into a line while first-order resistance, conductance, storage, and tangential-flow traces remain attached.
background.whyNow: The viewer must see what survives the geometric collapse before judging whether one-dimensional interface behavior was assumed or derived.
background.boundary: The reduction is first order by design and is not an exact replacement for a finite-width two-dimensional domain.
background.locator: PDF page 2, Section 3.1, Equations (1)-(4b) and Figure 1
background.sourceExcerpt: A thin layer, first order re-derivation shows that both our interface and the Anderson
background.visualObject: A finite-width fault strip collapsing onto a Wentzell-Robin interface with retained first-order traces.
background.motionPurpose: Make approximation order visible as geometry disappears but leading contributions persist.
background.stepDetail: Narrow the strip continuously; keep the leading interface traces at full opacity; fade the higher-order thickness bands.
background.transitionBridge: The surviving traces become the inputs to the thin-layer derivation.

methods.input: The full two-dimensional drawdown field inside a fault of width w_f.
methods.operation: Expand the fault drawdown about one interface
methods.output: A Taylor series ordered by powers of w_f.
methods.validity: The fault width is small relative to the adjacent-aquifer characteristic length.
methods.sourceExcerpt: Consider the drawdown within the fault is expanded in a Taylor series
methods.visualObject: A drawdown profile across the fault with value, slope, and curvature terms peeled into separate layers.
methods.motionPurpose: Turn the cross-fault profile into thickness-ordered contributions.
methods.stepDetail: Pin the profile at the left interface; draw its tangent; separate the value, linear, and curvature contributions by powers of fault width.
methods.transitionBridge: The ordered layers permit the higher-order geometry to be removed explicitly.

methods.input: The thickness-ordered Taylor expansion of fault drawdown.
methods.output: A Wentzell dynamic flux-jump condition on the zero-thickness interface.
methods.validity: Terms of order w_f^2 and higher are consistently omitted in the thin-layer limit.
methods.sourceExcerpt: This is equivalent to the imperfect interface condition for the flux jump
methods.visualObject: The linear Taylor layer folds onto the interface and becomes a normal-flux jump bracket.
methods.motionPurpose: Show that the interface condition is the visible remainder of the truncation.
methods.stepDetail: Fade the curvature layer; preserve the value and slope layers; fold them onto the interface; attach the resulting flux-jump bracket.
methods.transitionBridge: The bracket raises the remaining question of whether tangential variation was lost.

methods.input: The full fault-zone flow equation across the interval from 0 to w_f.
methods.operation: Integrate the equation across the fault width
methods.output: A conductive-fault boundary whose averaged drawdown retains shear-flux along the interface.
methods.validity: The integrated balance preserves the tangential term through the average drawdown even after the width is collapsed.
methods.locator: PDF page 2, Section 3.2, Equation (5) and Figure 2
methods.sourceExcerpt: the conductive-fault boundary discussed in Anderson (2006) also characterizes the shear-flux along the
methods.visualObject: Tangential gradient traces on both fault faces merge into a symmetric cross-sectional average trace.
methods.motionPurpose: Reveal where the apparently missing two-dimensional information resides in the interface variable.
methods.stepDetail: Sweep a bracket across the fault width; collect the two face gradients; merge them into the average-drawdown curve along the interface.
methods.transitionBridge: The derived interface can now be tested against a fault that retains its full width.

methods.input: A three-zone aquifer-fault-aquifer domain with finite fault width, anisotropic coefficients, pumping, initial conditions, and interface continuity.
methods.operation: Solve the transient full two-dimensional reference problem
methods.output: A finite-width reference drawdown field for the aquifer and fault zones.
methods.validity: The reference explicitly resolves two-dimensional flow within the fault and enforces drawdown and normal-flux continuity at both fault faces.
methods.sourceExcerpt: To validate this first-order approximation, we applied a full 2D solution of the three-zone model as a reference
methods.visualObject: The interface line reopens into a finite fault subdomain carrying a resolved two-dimensional drawdown field.
methods.motionPurpose: Produce an independent comparison field that does not discard fault width.
methods.stepDetail: Reopen the interface; restore the fault material coefficients; enforce continuity at both faces; propagate pumping drawdown through all three zones.
methods.transitionBridge: The resolved field aligns beside the interface field for pointwise comparison.

methods.input: Paired interface and full-reference drawdown values over all grid points, times, width ratios, and anisotropy ratios.
methods.output: Scenario-specific RMSE values for interface-to-reference drawdown differences.
methods.validity: The paper computes RMSE over the same co-registered spatial comparison interval for each thickness and anisotropy scenario.
methods.sourceExcerpt: root mean square error (RMSE) values computed over
methods.visualObject: Co-registered interface and reference contours collapsing into one RMSE marker for each scenario
methods.motionPurpose: Reduce each paired field comparison to a scenario-level discrepancy measure.
methods.stepDetail: Align the two fields; form pointwise differences; square them; average over the comparison grid; reveal the RMSE marker.
methods.transitionBridge: Pointwise relative errors from all scenarios are assembled into distributions in M06.

methods.input: Absolute relative drawdown errors from all grid points times and anisotropy scenarios.
methods.output: Kernel-density error distributions against 5% and 10% tolerances.
methods.validity: The paper aggregates the pointwise error dataset across the stated spatial grid and transient scenarios before estimating its density.
methods.sourceExcerpt: kernel density estimates of the absolute relative drawdown
methods.visualObject: Pointwise error clouds resolving into density curves crossed by 5% and 10% tolerance lines
methods.motionPurpose: Expose what fraction of the tested domain lies within each engineering tolerance.
methods.stepDetail: Gather the pointwise errors; form one density per width ratio; draw the 5% line; draw the 10% line; shade the accepted mass.
methods.transitionBridge: The threshold crossings map directly back onto fault thickness and transient regime.

symbols.visualObject: Brace spanning the finite fault strip
symbols.transition: The brace contracts with the fault strip and remains as a width label.

symbols.visualObject: Fault-width brace divided by the well-to-fault distance arrow
symbols.transition: The two geometric lengths transform into a ratio label beside the domain.

symbols.visualObject: Color field of dimensionless pumping drawdown
symbols.transition: A color-scale swatch becomes the MathTex field label.

symbols.visualObject: Pointwise gap between aligned interface and reference drawdown values
symbols.transition: The visible pointwise gap moves to the numerator and the reference value becomes the denominator.

return.visualObject: The density curves and tolerance lines fold back onto the original finite fault, coloring thin and moderate widths as interface-suitable and the thick early-time anisotropic regime as explicit-2D territory.
return.claim: For the tested parameter combinations, the first-order interface reproduces full two-dimensional drawdown within customary engineering tolerances over most of the domain when the fault-width ratio is at or below one.
return.claimBoundary: Larger deviations occur for markedly thick faults and at very early times under strong anisotropy; the benchmark covers the paper's tested widths, times, anisotropy ratios, and flow assumptions rather than every natural fault.
return.locator: PDF pages 5-7, Section 3.3.5, Figure 8, and Section 4
return.sourceExcerpt: Larger deviations mainly arise at very early times under strong anisotropy or when the fault is markedly thick.

visual.primaryObject: Pumped aquifer-fault-aquifer cross-section with a fault that transforms between finite width and an interface
visual.accentObject: Absolute-relative-error density with 5% and 10% tolerance lines
visual.measuredQuantity: Dimensionless drawdown and its approximation error
visual.geometryNotes: Keep the well and fault orientation fixed while the fault strip narrows to a line; preserve normal and tangential directions, compare co-registered drawdown fields, and return the error thresholds to the fault-width geometry.
